content.text: Why Head Sampling Fails GenAI Workloads

Head sampling — deciding at trace start whether to record — is the simplest sampling strategy, but it fails for GenAI in two ways. First, you cannot know at request start whether the call will error, hit a content filter, or take 45 seconds: all that information emerges only after the LLM response completes. Second, GenAI traffic is naturally bursty; a 1% head sample at steady state may drop to 0.1% during a traffic spike, losing all signal exactly when you need it most.

Tail sampling solves both problems: the sampling decision is deferred until the full trace is available, so every error, every outlier latency, and every content filter hit can be kept unconditionally — while healthy fast traces are sampled at a configurable rate.

attrs.alt: Tail Sampling Decision Pipeline for GenAI

content.text: How the OTel Collector Tail Sampling Processor Works
processor buffers all spans belonging to a trace in memory until a configurable
window (typically 10–30 seconds). Once the window closes — or all spans for the trace arrive — policies are evaluated against the complete trace. The trace is kept if
policy matches (OR logic by default). Only then are spans forwarded to the exporter.
This approach has one significant cost: the collector must hold spans in RAM for up to

seconds for every active trace. For a 50K req/s service with P99 latency 8 seconds, that buffer can reach tens of millions of spans. Sizing the collector fleet correctly is critical — under-provisioning causes the processor to drop traces or flush decisions early.

processors:
  tail_sampling:
    # How long to wait before making a decision (must exceed P99 latency)
    decision_wait: 10s
    # How many traces to hold in the decision cache simultaneously
    num_traces: 500000
    # How often to evaluate policies against buffered traces
    expected_new_traces_per_sec: 50000

    policies:
      # 1. Always keep error traces
      - name: error_status
        type: status_code
        status_code: { status_codes: [ERROR] }

      # 2. Always keep slow traces (LLM latency outliers)
      - name: slow_request
        type: latency
        latency: { threshold_ms: 10000 }

      # 3. Always keep content-filtered traces
      - name: content_filtered
        type: string_attribute
        string_attribute:
          key: gen_ai.finish_reason
          values: ["content_filter", "safety"]

      # 4. Sample a fixed rate of healthy traces for baseline
      - name: healthy_baseline
        type: rate_limiting
        rate_limiting: { spans_per_second: 100 }

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [memory_limiter, batch, tail_sampling]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]
Span status equals ERROR or UNSET
Catch all LLM call failures, provider errors
Capture slow responses: token stream hang, large context
Keep N spans/second regardless of policy
Alternative to rate_limiting for burst-tolerant sampling
Tiered rules: always error, then rate-limit others
Applied per model via routing connector
Standard sampling policies are designed for web services with uniform span attributes. GenAI workloads require additional policies keyed to the

policies:
  # Keep all traces where total token count is very high (expensive calls)
  - name: large_context
    type: numeric_attribute
    numeric_attribute:
      key: gen_ai.usage.total_tokens
      min_value: 8000

  # Keep traces with high cost (catch expensive multi-step agents)
  - name: high_cost_call
    type: numeric_attribute
    numeric_attribute:
      key: gen_ai.cost_usd
      min_value: 0.10

  # Keep all streaming traces (more complex, higher debugging value)
  - name: streaming_request
    type: boolean_attribute
    boolean_attribute:
      key: gen_ai.streaming
      value: true

Different model tiers warrant different sampling rates. New, expensive frontier models need 100% sampling for the first 30 days to build baseline distribution data. Mature, low-cost models can be sampled at 1–2%.

policies:
  # New or frontier model: sample everything for observability baseline
  - name: frontier_model_full
    type: string_attribute
    string_attribute:
      key: gen_ai.request.model
      values: ["gpt-4o", "claude-3-5-sonnet", "gemini-1.5-pro"]

  # Legacy small model: very light sampling, errors only + 0.5%
  - name: legacy_model_baseline
    type: and
    and:
      and_sub_policy:
        - name: is_legacy
          type: string_attribute
          string_attribute:
            key: gen_ai.request.model
            values: ["gpt-3.5-turbo", "claude-instant"]
        - name: low_rate
          type: rate_limiting
          rate_limiting: { spans_per_second: 5 }

Traces triggered by automated eval runs (LLM-as-judge, regression suites) carry a distinct attribute. Keeping 100% of eval traces lets you correlate eval score drift directly with trace-level span data.

- name: eval_trace_full
    type: string_attribute
    string_attribute:
      key: gen_ai.eval.run_id
      # Matches any non-empty value (eval attribute present)
      enabled_regex_matching: true
      values: [".+"]

Under-sizing the tail sampling collector is the most common operational failure. When memory pressure forces early decisions, the processor drops the oldest buffered spans — usually the beginning of long-running agentic traces — and logs
The memory required to buffer traces for tail sampling is:

# Rough memory budget for tail sampling collector
requests_per_second = 50_000
decision_wait_seconds = 10         # must exceed P99 trace duration
spans_per_trace = 8                # avg for single LLM call with context
bytes_per_span = 2_000             # OTel span with gen_ai.* attrs, ~2KB

# Peak concurrent traces in buffer
buffered_traces = requests_per_second * decision_wait_seconds
# = 50,000 * 10 = 500,000 traces

# Memory for spans
span_memory_gb = (buffered_traces * spans_per_trace * bytes_per_span) / 1e9
# = 500,000 * 8 * 2,000 / 1e9 = 8 GB

# Add 2x safety margin (span size variance, overhead)
required_memory_gb = span_memory_gb * 2
print(f"Collector memory target: {required_memory_gb:.0f} GB")
# → 16 GB per collector instance
A single collector instance cannot hold all in-flight traces. Multiple instances require a load balancing exporter to route
all spans for the same trace to the same instance
. Without this, the tail sampler never sees a complete trace and makes incorrect decisions.

# Upstream gateway collector — routes by traceID hash
exporters:
  loadbalancing:
    protocol:
      otlp:
        tls: { insecure: true }
    resolver:
      static:
        hostnames:
          - tail-sampler-0.internal:4317
          - tail-sampler-1.internal:4317
          - tail-sampler-2.internal:4317
    # Hash routing: same trace ID → same backend
    routing_key: traceID

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [batch]
      exporters: [loadbalancing]
Each tail-sampler instance then applies the full policy set against the complete trace it holds. The Kubernetes deployment uses
to spread instances across nodes, and PodDisruptionBudgets to prevent more than one instance being evicted simultaneously (which would lose all buffered traces on that node).

processor must run before the tail sampler in the pipeline. It tracks heap usage and signals backpressure upstream when a threshold is crossed, forcing the load balancer to slow sending rather than letting the collector OOM.

processors:
  memory_limiter:
    # Begin refusing data when heap exceeds 80% of limit
    limit_percentage: 80
    # Recover when heap drops below 70%
    spike_limit_percentage: 25
    check_interval: 1s

  tail_sampling:
    decision_wait: 10s
    num_traces: 500000
    # ... policies

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      processors: [memory_limiter, batch, tail_sampling]
      #            ^^ must be FIRST to enable backpressure
When different model families need fundamentally different sampling policies, a routing connector splits the trace stream before the tail sampler, letting each sub-pipeline carry its own policy set:

connectors:
  routing/model_tier:
    default_pipelines: [traces/standard]
    error_mode: ignore
    table:
      - statement: route() where attributes["gen_ai.request.model"] == "gpt-4o"
        pipelines: [traces/frontier]
      - statement: route() where attributes["gen_ai.request.model"] matches "gpt-3.5.*"
        pipelines: [traces/legacy]

service:
  pipelines:
    # Ingress — splits to per-tier pipelines
    traces/input:
      receivers: [otlp]
      exporters: [routing/model_tier]

    # Frontier: 100% sampling
    traces/frontier:
      receivers: [routing/model_tier]
      processors: [tail_sampling/frontier_full]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]

    # Standard: errors + 5%
    traces/standard:
      receivers: [routing/model_tier]
      processors: [tail_sampling/standard]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]

    # Legacy: errors only + 0.5%
    traces/legacy:
      receivers: [routing/model_tier]
      processors: [tail_sampling/legacy]
      exporters: [otlp/tempo]
Two collector metrics indicate how well the tail sampling policy is working. Both are exported via the collector's internal telemetry endpoint.
Time to evaluate all policies per trace
> 0 → attribute key mismatch or config bug
Spans that arrived after decision window closed
Spans refused by memory limiter backpressure
> 0 → need more collector RAM or instances

# Effective sampling rate (keep / total evaluated)
rate(processor_tail_sampling_count_traces_sampled{sampled="true"}[5m])
/
rate(processor_tail_sampling_count_traces_sampled[5m])

# Alert: sampling rate dropped below 1% (policy too aggressive)
- alert: TailSamplingRateTooLow
  expr: |
    rate(processor_tail_sampling_count_traces_sampled{sampled="true"}[5m])
    / rate(processor_tail_sampling_count_traces_sampled[5m])
    < 0.01
  for: 10m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Tail sampling keeping fewer than 1% of traces"
    runbook_url: "https://runbooks.example.com/tail-sampling-rate-low"

For very high-volume services (> 100K req/s), a two-stage approach reduces collector memory pressure: a probabilistic head sampler at the SDK discards a fixed fraction of requests unconditionally before any spans reach the collector, and the tail sampler then applies quality-based policies to what remains.

# SDK-side: probabilistic head sampler at 60% — drops 40% before export
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.sampling import TraceIdRatioBased, ParentBased

# ParentBased ensures downstream spans respect upstream sampling decision
sampler = ParentBased(root=TraceIdRatioBased(0.60))

from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
provider = TracerProvider(sampler=sampler)

# IMPORTANT: the tail sampler in the collector sees only 60% of traces.
# Its rate_limiting policy should be calibrated accordingly:
#   target_keep_rate_overall = 0.05 (5%)
#   head_sample_rate = 0.60
#   tail_rate_limiting_target = 0.05 / 0.60 = 0.083 (8.3% of what arrives)

Warning: combining head and tail sampling introduces a bias. Errors and outliers that happen to be in the 40% discarded by the head sampler are never seen by the tail sampler — they are lost entirely. Only use head+tail when your error rate is low enough that a 40% loss of error traces is acceptable, or configure the SDK to always sample traces that start with an error.
goes non-zero, the following sequence diagnoses and recovers:
— if > 90% of pod memory limit, the memory_limiter is correctly refusing data. Scale up the pod memory limit or add instances.
is shorter than real trace duration. Increase
ratio — if sampling rate is very high (> 20%), a policy is too broad. Review
. If spans for the same traceID are arriving at different tail-sampler instances, the load balancer
is misconfigured or the instances changed after a rollout.
to 5s. This forces early decisions with incomplete traces but prevents further OOM pressure. Increase RAM before raising it again.

Tail sampling defers the keep/drop decision until a complete trace is available, making it the correct strategy for GenAI workloads where errors, latency outliers, and content filter hits are unknowable at request start. The OTel Collector's
processor evaluates policies against the full trace after a configurable wait window:
provides a statistical baseline of healthy traffic. Effective operation requires sizing collector memory to hold all in-flight traces for the full
window, routing all spans for each trace to the same instance via the
processor first in the pipeline to signal backpressure before OOM occurs.
